perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and each individual Non-Disclosure Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to conflicts of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and each individual Non-Disclosure Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, giving full effect to conflicts of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to give effect to conflicts of law principles introduces uncertainty because Delaware law typically applies its own statutes unless a specific exception exists. For the contract to specifically include 'giving full effect to conflicts of law principles' creates a direct contradiction with the accepted standard of Delaware conflict of law rules that often prioritize Delaware law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law generally applies its own statutes unless specific exceptions apply. This modification directly contradicts Delaware choice of law principles, which do not always give 'full effect' to conflicts of law principles, particularly in contractual disputes.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that it is governed by the laws of Delaware, "without regard to conflicts of law principles." The modified version changes this to "giving full effect to conflicts of law principles." According to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, Delaware law typically applies its own statutes unless a specific exception exists. The changed text creates a direct contradiction with the standard application of Delaware law, which does not always give full effect to conflicts of law principles, particularly in contractual disputes. The scraped snippets, while not directly addressing conflict of law principles, establish that Delaware law should be interpreted according to its own statutes unless inconsistent with the intent of the General Assembly (Snippet 1). The change in the contract mandates giving 'full effect' to conflict of laws principles, which would contradict the typical application of Delaware law.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure or use of the Confidential Information would cause irreparable harm and significant injury to the Disclosing Party, the amount of which may be difficult to ascertain and which may not be adequately compensated at law.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure or use of the Confidential Information would cause harm to the Disclosing Party, the amount of which will never be difficult to ascertain and which may always be adequately compensated at law.
perturbation.explanation: This modification contradicts the concept of 'irreparable harm,' a key element for injunctive relief. By stating that the harm will 'never be difficult to ascertain' and 'may always be adequately compensated at law', the agreement suggests that monetary damages are sufficient, thus undermining the possibility of obtaining an injunction, which is a common remedy in NDA cases.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 65 governs injunctions. A key requirement is demonstrating irreparable harm that cannot be adequately compensated by monetary damages. The modified text explicitly states the opposite, thus contradicting the basis for injunctive relief.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the established legal principle that 'irreparable harm' is a prerequisite for injunctive relief, as described in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies this by stating that the modified agreement now suggests monetary damages are sufficient, thus precluding injunctive relief. While the provided law snippets don't explicitly define 'irreparable harm' or detail the requirements for injunctive relief, Snippet 1 (Rule 65) focuses on 'injunctions and restraining orders' and mentions 'immediate and irreparable injury,' indicating the importance of 'irreparable injury' within the context of injunctions. The changed text asserts harm will 'never be difficult to ascertain' and 'may always be adequately compensated at law,' which negates the possibility of irreparable harm, directly conflicting with the basis for seeking an injunction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous communications and proposals, whether oral or written.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes a partial agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and does not supersede all prior or contemporaneous communications and proposals, whether oral or written.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause is an integration clause designed to ensure that only the terms within the contract are binding. By changing 'entire agreement' to 'partial agreement' and stating it does not supersede prior communications, the modification creates ambiguity regarding what constitutes the complete agreement and invites disputes based on parol evidence, which contradicts the function of a standard integration clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-202, the Parol Evidence Rule, generally prevents the introduction of prior or contemporaneous evidence to contradict a fully integrated agreement. The modified text suggests the agreement is not fully integrated and allows for such contradictory evidence, undermining the purpose of the rule.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The original text includes an integration clause designed to ensure the contract is the complete agreement. The changed text weakens this by stating it's only a 'partial agreement' and 'does not supersede' prior communications. This undermines the integration clause and potentially allows outside evidence to be considered, contradicting the purpose of an integration clause. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly define the UCC 2-202 Parol Evidence Rule, Snippet 2 mentions the UCC's role in standardizing commercial laws. The Perturbation explanation says this action contradicts the function of a standard integration clause. The Law Explanation mentions UCC § 2-202 and its purpose, supporting the determination that the modification creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be executed in counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, but all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may not be executed in counterparts, and any individual copy shall be deemed a draft with no legal effect until a single, unified document is created and signed by all parties.
perturbation.explanation: The clause regarding execution in counterparts is a standard provision allowing parties to sign separate copies of the agreement, which are then treated as one original document. By stating that counterparts are not allowed and are merely drafts, the modification introduces uncertainty about the validity of the agreement if signed in separate copies, contradicting standard contract practice and potentially invalidating the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: UETA § 9 validates electronic signatures and contracts executed in counterparts. By disallowing counterpart execution, the modified text contradicts the legal acceptance of this common practice, potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable if signed in separate copies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Archives & Special Collections • Library • Penn Carey Law Skip to main content Home Library » Archives & Special Collections Archives & Special Collections The Archives and Special Collections Department of Biddle Law Library preserves, promotes, and provides access to the papers and records of two major legal organizations: The American Law Institute (ALI), and the National Bankruptcy Archives. The Department also cares for a collection of rare books, manuscripts, and archives from the University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School. Search Center Skip Sidebar Navigation Library Expand mobile section navigation Research Using The Library Faculty Services Archives & Special Collections About the Library News Library Map Library Hours Contact Us Collections The Archives of the American Law Institute (ALI) The ALI was established in 1923 by judges, lawyers, and law professors in an effort to promote the clarification, simplification, and scholarship of the law. The ALI Archives contains over 800 cubic feet of material documenting the administration, organization and work of the Institute, from early projects like the restatement of basic legal subjects to a collaboration with NCCUSL in developing and monitoring the Uniform Commercial Code. View more information about the ALI collections The National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA) In 2000, the Biddle Law Library and the American College of Bankruptcy collaborated to establish the National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA), a national repository of materials relating to the history of debtor-creditor relations, bankruptcy, and the reorganization of debt. The NBA currently contains over 1,000 linear feet of personal papers and institutional records. View more information about the NBA collections Explore the NBA Oral History collections Biddle Collections The Collections consist of manuscript collections, rare books, oral histories, and a select number of Penn Carey Law School’s records. The manuscript collections include the papers of George Wharton Pepper, Bernie Segal, William Draper Lewis, and David L. Bazelon. The Raymond F. Trent Collection consists of books, periodicals, articles, and audio tapes concerning the history of education and practice of Black lawyers in the United States. Some records relating to the operations of Penn Carey Law School are maintained by the University Archives . Please contact us for more information. Remote Research For patrons who cannot travel to the Law School to conduct research, the Archives will assist in the identification of potentially relevant materials for your research topic or for photocopying. perturbation.justification: The original text allows for execution in counterparts, a standard practice. The changed text explicitly disallows this, stating that any individual copy is a draft with no legal effect. The Law Explanation states that UETA § 9 validates contracts executed in counterparts. Although no specific snippet is given to confirm this, the explanation indicates that disallowing counterpart execution contradicts the acceptance of this common practice. The Perturbation Explanation also states that the modification introduces uncertainty about the validity of the agreement if signed in separate copies, contradicting standard contract practice.
